metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.course_name: EFP Literary Studies and Spoken Language 11
metadata.description: EFP Literary Studies and Spoken Language 11 integrates literary analysis with exploration of Indigenous oral traditions, oratory, and spoken word. Students study First Peoples literature while examining how Indigenous oral traditions, storytelling practices, and contemporary spoken word poetry represent literary expression. This course emphasizes the connections between written and oral forms, the cultural significance of storytelling in Indigenous communities, and the power of spoken language as a literary art form. Through analysis and performance, students develop appreciation for diverse Indigenous literary voices and practice their own oral expression grounded in literary understanding.
metadata.general_description: An integrated exploration of Indigenous literature and oral traditions, examining written and spoken forms of storytelling and literary expression.

big_ideas.statement: Indigenous oral traditions and contemporary literature are inseparable expressions of a living literary culture that values spoken and written word equally.
big_ideas.elaboration: First Peoples oral narratives, spoken word poetry, and written literature form an integrated literary heritage. Traditional oral traditions continue to influence contemporary writing, and contemporary Indigenous literature often incorporates oral narrative structures and sensibilities. Understanding both forms enriches appreciation for the depth and continuity of Indigenous literary expression.

big_ideas.statement: Spoken word and oral performance are sophisticated literary arts that employ distinctive techniques and carry profound cultural meaning and responsibility.
big_ideas.elaboration: Indigenous speakers, poets, storytellers, and orators craft literature orally using rhythm, tone, silence, repetition, and call-and-response patterns. Oral performance is not merely recitation of written texts but a distinct literary form with its own aesthetics, protocols, and cultural significance. Understanding oral literature requires attending to how meaning is created through sound, delivery, and audience relationship.

big_ideas.statement: Literary analysis of Indigenous texts, whether oral or written, reveals how authors express worldviews, maintain cultural knowledge, and respond to historical and contemporary issues.
big_ideas.elaboration: Close reading and listening to Indigenous literature uncovers layers of meaning—narrative structures, symbolism, historical allusions, and cultural references. Analyzing both oral and written forms develops sophisticated understanding of how Indigenous writers and speakers craft arguments, build emotional impact, and communicate with audiences.

big_ideas.statement: Performance of Indigenous literature—whether reading poetry aloud or practicing storytelling—connects readers/performers to literary traditions and deepens engagement with texts.
big_ideas.elaboration: Performing literature aloud brings texts to life, revealing dimensions that silent reading alone cannot access. Through performance, students embody literary voices, experience the oral artistry of language, and participate in traditions of shared storytelling. Performance also builds confidence and deepens personal connection to literary content.

categories.name: Comprehend and Connect
categories.raw: Students analyze Indigenous literature in both written and oral forms, understanding connections between these modes and the cultural meanings embedded in literary expression.

competencies.raw: Conduct sophisticated analysis of Indigenous literature, examining literary techniques, themes, and cultural significance across written and oral forms
i_can_statements: I can analyze literary devices, narrative structure, and characterization in Indigenous written literature with depth
I can identify distinctive techniques and structures in Indigenous oral narratives and spoken word
I can explain how written and oral versions of Indigenous stories compare and why differences matter
I can trace thematic connections between oral traditions and contemporary Indigenous literature

competencies.raw: Understand how Indigenous oral traditions, storytelling protocols, and speaking practices shape literary expression and cultural identity
i_can_statements: I can explain the cultural protocols and responsibilities associated with different Indigenous stories and who can tell them
I can identify storytelling techniques and oral literature structures used by Indigenous speakers and writers
I can analyze how language choices, tone, and rhythm create meaning in Indigenous oral and written literature
I can discuss the relationship between oral traditions and community identity, values, and knowledge systems

competencies.raw: Make connections between Indigenous literature, personal experience, and broader themes of identity, resilience, land connection, and cultural continuity
i_can_statements: I can analyze how Indigenous literature expresses themes of cultural identity, resistance, and resilience
I can relate literary content to my own experiences and perspectives
I can discuss how Indigenous literature contributes to cultural preservation and community wellbeing
I can reflect on how engaging with Indigenous literary voices deepens understanding of First Peoples worldviews

categories.name: Create and Communicate
categories.raw: Students develop literary analyses, practice oral performance, and create their own spoken or written literary work grounded in Indigenous traditions.

competencies.raw: Produce sophisticated written literary analyses of Indigenous texts in both oral and written forms
i_can_statements: I can write detailed essays examining Indigenous literature, its techniques, themes, and cultural contexts
I can use textual evidence from both oral and written sources to support literary analysis
I can develop original arguments about Indigenous literature and articulate them persuasively
I can conduct research on Indigenous authors, oral traditions, and literary movements with analytical depth

competencies.raw: Perform Indigenous literature—reading poetry aloud, storytelling, or spoken word—with attention to oral artistry and audience engagement
i_can_statements: I can read Indigenous poetry and literature aloud with appropriate pace, tone, and expression
I can perform storytelling that honors oral tradition protocols and conventions
I can adapt written literature for oral performance, making intentional choices about voice and presentation
I can practice spoken word performance that conveys meaning and emotion effectively to audiences

competencies.raw: Create original literary work—spoken word, poetry, narratives—that draws on Indigenous literary traditions and expresses distinctive voice
i_can_statements: I can write poetry or spoken word that incorporates Indigenous storytelling elements and cultural references
I can craft narratives that reflect Indigenous worldviews and express authentic voice
I can develop literary work that engages with themes significant to Indigenous communities and experiences
I can perform or present my literary work, receiving and incorporating feedback for revision

areas.name: Indigenous Oral Traditions and Storytelling Practices
areas.raw: Study of First Peoples oral narratives, storytelling protocols, and the cultural significance of oral communication in Indigenous literary traditions
topics: Creation stories and mythological narratives in Indigenous traditions
Teaching stories and wisdom narratives
Storytelling protocols, responsibilities, and cultural permissions
Oral narrative structures and distinctive features
Regional variations in oral traditions across First Peoples nations
Transmission and preservation of Indigenous stories across generations

areas.name: Indigenous Literature: Written Forms and Authors
areas.raw: Analysis of Indigenous written literature across genres, examining distinctive voices, themes, and literary techniques employed by First Peoples authors
topics: Indigenous novelists and major literary works
Indigenous poets and poetry collections
Memoir, autobiography, and personal narrative by First Peoples writers
Short stories and literary journals by Indigenous authors
Plays and dramatic literature by Indigenous playwrights
Literary movements and contemporary trends in Indigenous writing

areas.name: Spoken Word, Oratory, and Performance Poetry
areas.raw: Examination of Indigenous speakers, poets, and performers who use oral expression as a literary art form; study of performance techniques and contemporary Indigenous spoken word
topics: Traditional Indigenous oratory and eloquent speakers
Contemporary Indigenous spoken word poetry and slam poetry
Performance techniques: tone, rhythm, pacing, and physical presence
Indigenous hip-hop and musical poetry
Indigenous activists and educators using spoken language
Poetry reading and literary performance as art form

areas.name: Connections Between Oral Traditions and Contemporary Literature
areas.raw: Analysis of how contemporary Indigenous writers draw on oral tradition structures, themes, and sensibilities; examination of how literature maintains cultural knowledge and identity
topics: Oral narrative structures in written Indigenous literature
How contemporary Indigenous writers honor and adapt traditional stories
Language, imagery, and symbolism rooted in Indigenous worldviews
Themes of resilience, identity, and cultural continuity
Literature as cultural preservation and knowledge transmission
Indigenous literature and responses to colonialism and ongoing injustices
